notes.anchor: must creep secretly aboard some merchantman
notes.note: An oneraria was a broad-bellied sailing cargo ship, slower and less conspicuous than an oared galley; Cicero schemes to slip out of Italy on one to avoid the watch Antony has set on him.
Sicily, Rome's oldest province and a key grain source, was a strategic prize in the war; Cicero hoped to reach it as a stepping-stone toward Pompey before Caesar's man Curio secured the island.

notes.anchor: a rush of Sicilians to Cato begging him to make a stand
notes.note: M. Porcius Cato, the Stoic and staunchest of Pompey's partisans, held Sicily for the senatorial cause in early 49 BC but withdrew without a fight as Curio's force approached. Cicero doubts the report that the provincials had begged him to resist.

notes.anchor: Only let things go right in Spain!
notes.note: The Spanish provinces held Pompey's veteran legions under his legates; their fate was the war's pivot in 49 BC. Cicero's hopes hang on news from the front, which would in fact end in Caesar's victory at Ilerda.

notes.anchor: tear up at some point those letters of mine in which I wrote rather sharply
notes.note: Correspondence could be intercepted or fall into hostile hands in wartime, so Cicero asks Atticus to destroy candid letters about his nephew; the request shows the danger of frank writing under Caesarian surveillance.
